story.text: I have a simple question for you: What made you happy recently?

Everyone you see here answered this question as part of a [2017 research project](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/322695424_HappyDB_A_Corpus_of_100000_Crowdsourced_Happy_Moments). More than 10,000 people shared their happy moments.

I wanted to better understand what makes people happy. I thought it was important, because the world feels quite precarious right now. And sometimes I forget what I can do to be happier.


So that’s why I made…

Happy map

by alvin chang
Let me give you a quick tour.
In the **north ↑**, are experiences that fulfill **immediate** desires—things like eating your favorite food or going to the movies.
In the **south ↓** are experiences that take a **longer time** to come to fruition—things like receiving a college degree.
In the **← west ** side, are moments that we can’t trigger on our own—like receiving affection or a gift.
In the **east →** are experiences that we have more control over, often done alone—things like partaking in a hobby or buying a new car.
I’ll add a compass so you can stay oriented.

Researchers have found that these experiences tend to have a [short-term effect](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/psychology/hedonic-adaptation) on our happiness, because we [adapt](https://papers.ssrn.com/sol3/papers.cfm?abstract_id=956920) to various life events—both good and bad.
Historically, we [tend](https://www.pnas.org/doi/10.1073/pnas.1003744107) to be happy when we’re young, less happy in middle age, and then happy again when we’re old. [[ADD CHART]]
But these days, that’s not true. Researchers are [finding](https://journals.plos.org/plosone/article?id=10.1371/journal.pone.0327858) that young people are much less happy than before.
What’s interesting is that it’s easier than ever to get a quick hit of dopamine. Social media, delivery apps, and online dating means that we can have what we want almost instantly.
But when Harvard researchers [followed](https://www.adultdevelopmentstudy.org/grantandglueckstudy) people for their entire lives…
…what they found was that good relationships were critical for living happy, healthy lives.
Unfortunately, XXX Young People XXX. Social media / loneliness.
Researchers have also [found](https://penntoday.upenn.edu/news/does-more-money-correlate-greater-happiness-Penn-Princeton-research) that higher incomes lead to more happiness.
But XXXX Jobs / Housing / Economy XXXX.

Another critical thing that helps us be content is having a [meaningful life]((https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/abs/10.1080/17439760.2013.830764). This requires us to have a cohesive story about our past, present, and future.

But so much about our future seems precarious right now. The earth’s climate is becoming less hospitable to humans, we’re learning how corrupt our political leaders actually are, and it often feels like we’re hurtling toward more and more wars.

When experts and policymakers talk about happiness, they frame it in a desire to nudge the world so it can support more human happiness—more of what we want, rather than just making numbers go up on a screen. I mostly agree.

But increasingly, people are growing up in a world where it’s normal to order dopamine from a screen. I grew up well before smartphones and home internet—and even I forget what made me happy in the beforetimes.
So I’m starting to think we need to remind ourselves what durable happiness looks like—not just for ourselves, but for the world around us.
Explore these moments. Filter the responses by age, gender, and more using the button on the top-right.

info:
In 2017, more than 10,000 people tracked experiences that made them happy as part of a research project. The researchers gathered more than 100,000 happy moments.

Data methodology

I initially used the gemini-2.0-flash-001 LLM model to determine how much an agency score and time score (immediate vs. long-term), as well as a brief summary of the happy moment. These results helped me create a three-level topic taxonomy that is analogous to continents, countries, and states; there are obviously many ways to group these moments, so this is reflective of how I think about happy moments. I then prompted gemini-2.0-flash-001 to categorize each happy moment into the three-level taxonomy. I cleaned up the results in [Open Refine](https://openrefine.org/) to ensure there is no cross-taxonomy categorization; for example, the LLM sometimes put a moment into a state that is not within the proper parent country or continent. (This error is akin to saying that a moment belongs in New York state, and in the country of France.) From there, I used gemini-2.0-flash-001 to give each moment a score of how well it fits into its assigned categories, since sometimes moments didn't have a perfect category in the taxonomy; what you are more likely to see on the screen on the best fits.

Map methodology

The map is initially split up into 200,000 regions using a [voronoi](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Voronoi_diagram) algorithm; it looks like a giraffe's fur. From there, the top-level category—the “continents”—are initially given a starting atom, based on the average agency and time scores. It is then allowed to take over atoms based on the countries, states, and cities (moments) that it needs to fit within its bounds. That conquering process determined what the map looks like. This ultimately means that the moments mostly follow the x- and y-axis of time and agency, like a scatterplot. But since two things can't occupy the same space, it's not a perfect scatterplot. The moments are then placed on the map with illustrations of people. The people were drawn on paper with an ink pen and fountain pen, then digitized so they could be used as icons.

Citation

Akari Asai, Sara Evensen, Behzad Golshan, Alon Halevy, Vivian Li, Andrei Lopatenko, Daniela Stepanov, Yoshihiko Suhara, Wang-Chiew Tan, Yinzhan Xu, "HappyDB: A Corpus of 100,000 Crowdsourced Happy Moments", LREC '18, May 2018. | [Paper](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/322695424_HappyDB_A_Corpus_of_100000_Crowdsourced_Happy_Moments) | [Data](https://megagonlabs.github.io/HappyDB/)
text: But it seems harder to exist down here, where we can plan for a future in a functional and pro-social world.
